skill.name: backlog-manager
skill.description: 需求池管理。用户随时抛出想法/痛点，AI 负责追问、整理、合并、归档到需求池文件。用户准备开新版本时，协助从池中筛选。痛点驱动，不做提前排期。
skill.summary: AI-assisted backlog management skill that captures user pain points, organizes requirements into a living document, and helps prioritize features for development.
skill.author: yunshu0909

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged patterns that are false positives. The 'external_commands' detections reference file paths in documentation (docs/需求池.md), not actual command execution. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' warnings point to YAML frontmatter and workflow text, not crypto code. High entropy is due to mixed Chinese/English content. This is a documentation-based workflow guide with no executable code, network calls, or security-sensitive operations.

content.user_title: Manage Your Product Backlog with AI
content.value_statement: Capture product ideas and pain points effortlessly while AI organizes, merges, and prioritizes them into a living requirements document. Focus on what matters most without drowning in scattered notes.
actual_capabilities: Captures user pain points and feature ideas through structured questioning
Organizes requirements into four states: Ready to Build, Needs Thinking, On Hold, and Completed
Maintains a living backlog document with summary table and detailed sections
Merges related requirements and prevents duplicate entries
Analyzes requirements by frequency, workarounds, and ROI when prioritizing
Updates backlog status and archives completed features with version numbers
limitations: Requires manual user confirmation before modifying the backlog file
Does not automatically create designs or PRDs after requirement selection
Does not push users with reminders or automatic prioritization
Limited to text-based markdown file management without external integrations

use_cases.title: Solo Developer Feature Planning
use_cases.description: Individual developers capture ideas as they occur, then review and prioritize when ready to start a new version.

use_cases.title: Product Manager Requirements Hub
use_cases.description: PMs collect stakeholder requests and user feedback throughout the week, then systematically organize them for sprint planning.

use_cases.title: Small Team Backlog Coordination
use_cases.description: Small teams maintain a shared requirements document that tracks what has been completed and what is ready for development.
use_cases.target_user: Startup teams and small engineering groups

prompt_templates.title: Capture a New Requirement
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a pain point: [describe the problem]. Can you help me add this to my backlog?
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner - Quick capture of a new idea

prompt_templates.title: Review and Organize Backlog
prompt_templates.prompt: Let's review my backlog. Go through each item and suggest which ones should be promoted, deleted, or need more discussion.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize for Next Version
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm ready to start the next version. Show me all items marked 'Ready to Build' and help me analyze which one to tackle first based on frequency, workarounds, and ROI.

prompt_templates.title: Deep Dive on Complex Requirement
prompt_templates.prompt: Take this vague idea: [describe]. Ask me the right questions to clarify the pain point, frequency, current workarounds, and expected impact so we can properly define it.

output_examples.input: User says: 'I hate manually copying data between spreadsheets every week'
output: AI asks clarifying questions about frequency and current workaround
AI confirms understanding: 'You spend time weekly copying data manually between sheets'
AI suggests state: 'This sounds like Ready to Build - pain point is clear'
AI updates docs/需求池.md with new entry in summary table and detailed section
AI reads existing backlog and reviews each item
AI suggests promotions: 'Item #3 has enough detail to move from Needs Thinking to Ready to Build'
AI flags outdated items: 'Item #7 seems obsolete since the feature shipped in V0.5'
AI waits for user confirmation on each change before updating the file

best_practices: Always answer the three key questions: What's the pain point? How often does it occur? What's your current workaround?
Review your backlog every 2-4 weeks to prevent it from becoming stale or overwhelming
Keep requirements in the backlog until they are truly 'Ready to Build' - resist the urge to start design work prematurely
anti_patterns: Dumping vague ideas without going through the clarification questions
Letting the backlog grow to hundreds of items without periodic review and cleanup
Using the backlog as a todo list tracker instead of a requirements discovery tool

faq.question: What's the difference between this and a todo list?
faq.answer: A todo list tracks tasks you've already decided to do. This backlog captures raw pain points and ideas before you've decided whether to build them. It focuses on discovery and prioritization, not execution tracking.

faq.question: How do I know when a requirement is 'Ready to Build'?
faq.answer: A requirement is Ready to Build when you can clearly describe the pain point and have a rough idea of what the solution should look like. You don't need full specifications - just enough clarity that development could start.

faq.question: Can I use this for bug tracking?
faq.answer: This skill is designed for feature requests and product ideas, not bug tracking. Bugs should go into your issue tracker. However, if a recurring bug reveals a deeper product problem, that underlying problem could be a backlog item.

faq.question: What happens to completed requirements?
faq.answer: Completed items move to the 'Completed' section with a version number and completion date. They stay in the summary table for historical reference but are visually marked as done.

faq.question: Should I merge similar requirements or keep them separate?
faq.answer: The AI will suggest merging when new items relate to existing ones. Merge when they address the same underlying pain point. Keep separate when they solve different problems, even if the solutions might overlap.

faq.question: How often should I review my backlog?
faq.answer: Most users benefit from a review every 2-4 weeks. Review too frequently and nothing has changed. Review too infrequently and the backlog becomes overwhelming. Adjust based on how quickly you accumulate new items.
